message:
fix(signal_abi+string_abi): match glibc/x86_64 SIGRTMIN=34 and full strsignal real-time labeling

Two related ABI corrections that ride together because they share
the SIGRTMIN constant:

signal_abi.rs — __libc_current_sigrtmin

The function returned 35 with the comment "Linux reserves SIGRTMIN+0..+2
for NPTL; usable range starts at SIGRTMIN+3 = 35." That conflated
two different layers:

- The kernel's SIGRTMIN is 32 (raw kernel signals 32 and 33 are reserved
  for NPTL/glibc internals: SIGCANCEL=32, SIGSETXID=33).
- glibc's __libc_current_sigrtmin returns 34, which is the first signal
  number that user code is allowed to use after the two NPTL
  reservations.

Returning 35 was off by one — consumers calling SIGRTMIN+0 would
land on signal 35, but glibc-on-Linux/x86_64 makes SIGRTMIN+0 ==
34. Programs probing for real-time signal availability via the
documented "SIGRTMIN..SIGRTMAX" range would silently lose access
to one slot at the bottom.

Fix: return 34. Updated the inline comment to reflect the actual
glibc layer rather than the kernel-NPTL conflation.

string_abi.rs — strsignal real-time + unknown labeling

strsignal previously fell through to signal_name(sig) for any
sig outside the standard 1..=31 range, and signal_name returned
some catch-all string. Two issues:

1. Real-time signals (SIGRTMIN..=SIGRTMAX, glibc/Linux/x86_64
   range [34, 64]) were not labeled "Real-time signal N" the way
   glibc does it, so tools comparing strsignal() output for
   parity would diverge.
2. Truly unknown signals (negative, zero, the kernel-NPTL slot
   range 32..=33, anything above SIGRTMAX) were labeled with
   whatever signal_name's fallback returned, often empty or
   misleading.

Add two constants — GLIBC_SIGRTMIN=34, GLIBC_SIGRTMAX=64 —
matching the new __libc_current_sigrtmin/max return values, then
extend strsignal:

- 1..=31: existing signal_name path (SIGHUP, SIGINT, etc).
- GLIBC_SIGRTMIN..=GLIBC_SIGRTMAX: format
  "Real-time signal {sig - GLIBC_SIGRTMIN}" so SIGRTMIN+0 prints
  as "Real-time signal 0", matching the man page text.
- Anything else: "Unknown signal {sig}" so the user sees the raw
  number rather than an empty string.

The thread-local STRSIGNAL_BUF still bounds the output at 64
bytes; the formatted strings ("Real-time signal 30" is 19 bytes,
"Unknown signal -1" is 17 bytes) all fit comfortably under that.

tests/signal_abi_test.rs

- sigrtmin_returns_valid_value: tighten the assertion from
  contains(&rtmin) on [32, 40] to assert_eq!(rtmin, 34). The
  permissive range was hiding the off-by-one above; pinning to
  34 catches any future regression that re-introduces the NPTL
  conflation.

tests/string_abi_test.rs

- strsignal_realtime_signals_match_glibc_offsets: check 34, 35,
  64 → "Real-time signal 0", "Real-time signal 1", "Real-time
  signal 30" respectively.
- strsignal_unknown_signals_include_number: check -1, 0, 32, 33,
  65 → "Unknown signal {sig}". The 32 and 33 cases pin the
  NPTL-reserved slots out of the SIGRTMIN range as documented in
  the new constants.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
